hypotheses.title: Larger models outperform smaller ones on reasoning benchmarks
hypotheses.statement: Larger models (>7B parameters) achieve significantly higher accuracy on reasoning benchmarks compared to smaller models (≤1B parameters) across diverse reasoning tasks.
hypotheses.falsificationCriteria: If smaller models (≤1B params) match or exceed larger models (>7B params) on reasoning accuracy across diverse tasks, this hypothesis is falsified.
premises: Published results on GSM8K and MATH show scaling trends
Scaling laws predict monotonically improving performance with compute
Larger models have more capacity for multi-step reasoning chains
predictions: Larger models will show 5-15% higher accuracy on reasoning benchmarks
The advantage of scale grows with task complexity
Compute required for equivalent accuracy is non-linear with model size

hypotheses.title: Fine-tuning disproportionately helps smaller models
hypotheses.statement: Fine-tuning disproportionately benefits smaller models (≤1B) over larger models (>7B), narrowing the accuracy gap on reasoning benchmarks.
hypotheses.falsificationCriteria: If fine-tuning improves both model sizes by proportionally equal amounts (within ±2 percentage points), or if larger models benefit more in absolute gains, the hypothesis is falsified.
premises: Smaller models have more headroom for improvement
Capacity constraints in small models are partially alleviated by task-specific tuning
Larger models are already nearer to their performance ceiling in zero-shot settings
predictions: Smaller models will show 3-8x larger relative accuracy improvement from fine-tuning
The performance gap after fine-tuning narrows compared to zero-shot
Fine-tuned small models may approach pre-fine-tune large model performance
